response:
module 1: determine the equilibrium population size $ P_e $  
the equilibrium population size occurs when $ \frac{dP}{dt} = 0 $, so $ rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - h(t) = 0 $  
substituting $ r = 0.1 $, $ K = 1000 $, and $ h(t) = 10 + 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) $  
we solve $ 0.1P\left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) - 10 - 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) = 0 $  
this simplifies to $ P\left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) = 100 + 50\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) $  
the equilibrium population size $ P_e $ is the value of $ P $ that satisfies this equation for all $ t $  

module 2: analyze the stability of the equilibrium population size  
the stability of $ P_e $ depends on the sign of the derivative of the growth rate with respect to $ P $  
the derivative $ \frac{d}{dP} \left( rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - h(t) \right) = r\left(1 - \frac{2P}{K}\right) - 0 $  
this derivative is positive when $ P < \frac{K}{2} $ and negative when $ P > \frac{K}{2} $  
thus, $ P_e $ is stable if $ P_e < \frac{K}{2} $ and unstable if $ P_e > \frac{K}{2} $  

module 3: determine the time $ t $ when the population will no longer be sustainable  
the carrying capacity decreases linearly as $ K(t) = 1000 - 3t $  
the equilibrium population size $ P_e(t) $ is the value of $ P $ that satisfies $ 0.1P\left(1 - \frac{P}{1000 - 3t}\right) - 10 - 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right
